\NewDocumentCommand{\langfam}{m m g}{\textbf{#1$_{#2}$\IfNoValueF{#3}{#3}}}
\newcommand{\thislang}{\ensuremath{\mathcal{L}}}
\newcommand{\poly}{polynomial}
\newcommand{\nopolyunknownquasi}{not polynomial, quasipolynomial unknown}
\newcommand{\nopolyquasi}{not polynomial, quasipolynomial}
\newcommand{\unknownpolyquasi}{polynomial unknown, quasipolynomial}
\newcommand{\unknownboth}{unknown}
\newcommand{\noquasi}{not quasipolynomial}
\newcommand{\CO}{CO}
\newcommand{\VA}{VA}
\newcommand{\CE}{CE}
\newcommand{\IM}{IM}

\newcommand{\SE}{SE}

\newcommand{\SFO}{SFO}
\newcommand{\NOTC}{$\neg$C}
\newcommand{\ANDC}{$\wedge$C}
\newcommand{\ANDBC}{$\wedge$BC}
\newcommand{\ORC}{$\vee$C}
\newcommand{\ORBC}{$\vee$BC}
\newcommand{\compilespoly}[2]{#1 compiles to #2 with polynomial blowup}
\newcommand{\compilesquasi}[2]{#1 compiles to #2 with quasipolynomial blowup}
\newcommand{\nocompilespoly}[2]{#1 does not compile to #2 with polynomial blowup}
\newcommand{\nocompilesquasi}[2]{#1 does not compile to #2 with quasipolynomial blowup}
\newcommand{\supportspoly}[2]{#1 supports #2 in polynomial time}
\newcommand{\supportsquasi}[2]{#1 supports #2 in quasipolynomial time}
\newcommand{\nosupportspoly}[2]{#2 is not supported by #1 in polynomial time}
\newcommand{\nosupportsquasi}[2]{#2 is not supported by #1 in quasipolynomial time}
\newcommand{\isin}[1]{#1}

\newcommand{\fullname}[1]{\textbf{Full name:} #1\par}

\newcommand{\target}[1]{\textbf{Target:} #1\par}

\maketitle

% Status: GOOD
\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{CNF}}
\target{\langref{DNNF}}
\status{\noquasi}
\begin{description}
\citet[Theorem 1]{Bova_2014} gives a family of \langref{CNF} formulas whose smallest \langref{DNNF} representation has exponential size, so \langref{CNF} cannot compile to \langref{DNNF} in quasipolynomial size.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

% Status: CONSIDER DIFFERENT SOURCE
\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{CNF}}
\target{\langref{NNF}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
As defined by \citet{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{CNF} is a subset of \langref{NNF}. Therefore, a polynomial compilation is trivial.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

% Status: CONSIDER DIFFERENT SOURCE
\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{CNF}}
\target{\langref{PI}}
\status{\noquasi}
\begin{description}
It is well-known that some \langref{CNF} formulas (i.e. the negation of the parity function $\neg PARITY_n=\neg\bigoplus_{i=1}^n x_i$) have exponentially many prime implicants. \citep{Darwiche_2002}
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

% Status: GOOD
\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{cSDD}}
\target{\langref{SDD}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
For each fixed vtree $T$, \langfam{cSDD}{T} is the compressed subset of \langfam{SDD}{T}, so compilation between \langref{cSDD} and \langref{SDD} is trivial. \citep{VanDenBroeck_2015}
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}


% Status: GOOD
\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{cSDD}}
\target{\langref{OBDD}}
\status{\noquasi}
\begin{description}
\citet[Theorem 4]{Bova_2016_a} constructs a generalized hidden weighted bit function $F_n$ on $2n+1$ variables with \langref{cSDD} size $O(n^3)$ but \langref{OBDD} size $2^{\Omega(n)}$.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

% Status: NEED TO CANONICALIZE REF
\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{d-DNNF}}
\target{\langref{DNNF}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
As introduced in \citet{Darwiche_2001b}, \langref{d-DNNF} is a subset of \langref{DNNF} satisfying the determinism property, making a polynomial compilation trivial.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

% Status: Darwiche makes this claim in the KCM but it is missing from the arxiv version of the paper?
\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{d-DNNF}}
\target{\langref{FBDD}}
\status{\noquasi}
\begin{description}
\citet{Darwiche_2001a} provides an example of a d-DNNF sentence which only has exponential FBDD representations.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

% Status: GOOD, maybe update citation provenance
\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{d-DNNF}}
\target{\langref{MODS}}
\status{\noquasi}
\begin{description}
The $OR_n$ function $\bigvee_{i=1}^nx_i$ can be represented in $O(n)$ as a \langref{d-DNNF} formula but has $2^n-1$ models. \citep{Darwiche_2002}
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

% Status: GOOD
\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{d-SDNNF}}
\target{\langref{d-DNNF}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
As defined by \citet{Pipatsrisawat_Darwiche_2010}, a \langref{d-SDNNF} is simply a structured \langref{d-DNNF}, so discarding the vtree completes the compilation.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

% Status: GOOD
\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{d-SDNNF}}
\target{\langref{SDD}}
\status{\nopolyunknownquasi}
\begin{description}
\citet[Theorem 1]{Vinall-Smeeth_2024} proves that there are functions with structured deterministic \langref{DNNF} representations of size $s$ but whose every equivalent \langref{SDD} has size $s^{\widetilde{\Omega}(\log s)}$.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

% Status: GOOD
\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{d-SDNNF}}
\target{\langref{SDNNF}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
As defined by \citet{Pipatsrisawat_Darwiche_2010}, \langref{d-SDNNF} is exactly the subset of \langref{SDNNF} satisfying determinism, so compilation is trivial.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}


\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{d-SDNNF}}
\target{\langref{uOBDD}}
\status{\unknownpolyquasi}
\begin{description}
\citet[Theorem 2]{Bollig_2018} shows that \langref{SDNNF} can be compiled into \langref{nOBDD} by adapting the compilation of \citet{Beame_2015} from \langref{DNNF} to \langref{nFBDD}; \citet[Proposition 2]{Bollig_2018} shows that this \langref{nOBDD} is unambiguous if the \langref{SDNNF} is deterministic.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}
\target{\langref{d-SDNNF}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
\langref{d-SDNNF} is the union of the fixed-vtree languages \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}. Hence any \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} formula is already a \langref{d-SDNNF} formula, so the compilation is trivial and polynomial-time. \citep{Pipatsrisawat_2008}
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}
\target{\langref{nFBDD}}
\status{\nopolyunknownquasi}
\begin{description}
This follows because \langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T} is a polynomial-time sublanguage of \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} and \langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T} does not compile to \langref{nFBDD} with polynomial blowup.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}
\target{\langfam{SDD}{T}}
\status{\nopolyunknownquasi}
\begin{description}
\citet[Theorem 1 and proof of Theorem 2]{Vinall-Smeeth_2024} gives small source circuits that are explicitly structured: for the hard unambiguous \langref{DNF} $\psi'$, the proof fixes a vtree $T$ over $\operatorname{var}(\psi')$, compiles each term as a \langref{d-DNNF} respecting $T$, and takes their deterministic disjunction. Thus the source side can be stated as \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}. The lower bound is against every equivalent \langref{SDD}; since \langfam{SDD}{T} is a subclass of \langref{SDD}, it also rules out every target vtree class member.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}
\target{\langfam{SDNNF}{T}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
For any fixed vtree $T$, \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} is exactly the subset of \langfam{SDNNF}{T} satisfying determinism, so compilation is trivial. \citep{Amarilli_2018}
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}
\target{\langfam{uOBDD}{<}}
\status{\unknownpolyquasi}
\begin{description}
The common-order quasipolynomial simulation of \citet{TCZ_Forthcoming} specializes to \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}: for every fixed source vtree $T$, it chooses one output order $<$ depending only on $T$; determinism guarantees that the output is a \langfam{uOBDD}{<}.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{DNF}}
\target{\langref{d-DNNF}}
\status{\nopolyunknownquasi}
\assuming{the polynomial hierarchy does not collapse}
\begin{description}
Because counting the models of a \langref{d-DNNF} is in P and counting the models of a \langref{DNF} is \#P-complete, \langref{DNF} cannot be polynomially compiled into \langref{d-DNNF} unconditionally. Such a compilation implies P = \#P, collapsing the polynomial hierarchy \citep{Darwiche_2002}.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{DNF}}
\target{\langref{d-SDNNF}}
\status{\noquasi}
\begin{description}
\citet[Corollary 7.6]{Amarilli_2018} gives a lower bound of $2^{\Omega(tw(\phi))}$ on the smallest \langref{d-SDNNF} computing any monotone \langref{DNF} of constant arity and degree. In particular, \langref{DNF} families whose treewidth grows linearly with their size force exponential-size \langref{d-SDNNF} representations.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{DNF}}
\target{\langref{DNNF}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
As defined by \citet{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{DNF} is a subset of \langref{DNNF}. Therefore, a polynomial compilation is trivial.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{DNF}}
\target{\langref{IP}}
\status{\noquasi}
\begin{description}
It is well-known that some \langref{DNF} formulas (i.e. the parity function $PARITY_n=\bigoplus_{i=1}^n x_i$) have exponentially many prime implicates. \citep{Darwiche_2002}
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{DNF}}
\target{\langref{OBDD}}
\status{\noquasi}
\begin{description}
\citet{Devadas_1993} showed that there exist Boolean functions with polynomial-size \langref{DNF} representations but requiring exponential-size \langref{OBDD} representations for any variable ordering.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{DNF}}
\target{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
Fix any variable order $<$. Each term of a \langref{DNF} can be represented as an \langfam{OBDD}{<} of linear size (a chain of decision nodes for each literal in the term). Their nondeterministic disjunction yields an \langfam{nOBDD}{<} of size $O(|\phi|)$ because every branch follows the same fixed order. \citep{Amarilli_2018,Wegener_2000}
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{DNNF}}
\target{\langref{d-DNNF}}
\status{\noquasi}
\begin{description}
\citet[Proposition 7]{Bova_2016} shows that the Sauerhoff function $S_n$ has \langref{DNNF} size $O(n^2)$. Theorem 9 shows that $S_n$ has \langref{d-DNNF} size $2^{\Omega(n)}$. (Also see \citet{Bova_2014}.)
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{DNNF}}
\target{\langref{nFBDD}}
\status{\unknownpolyquasi}
\begin{description}
\citet[Section 5]{Bodlaender_1993} extends the compilation of \langref{dec-DNNF}s into \langref{FBDD}s (shown in \citet{Beame_2013}) to show that \langref{DNNF} can be quasipolynomial-time compiled into \langref{nFBDD}.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{DNNF}}
\target{\langref{NNF}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
As defined by \citet{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{DNNF} is a subset of \langref{NNF}, so a polynomial compilation is trivial.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{FBDD}}
\target{\langref{d-DNNF}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
As defined by \citet{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{FBDD} is a subset of \langref{d-DNNF}, so a polynomial compilation is trivial.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{FBDD}}
\target{\langref{SDD}}
\status{\noquasi}
\begin{description}
\citet{Beame_2015} relates the size of \langref{SDD} representations to best-partition communication complexity and derive simple examples for which \langref{FBDD} representations are exponentially more concise than \langref{SDD} representations.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{FBDD}}
\target{\langref{SDNNF}}
\status{\noquasi}
\begin{description}
\citet{Pipatsrisawat_2010} and \citet[Section 6.3]{Capelli_2016} independently proved this result using different techniques.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{FBDD}}
\target{\langref{uFBDD}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
As defined by \citet{Amarilli_2018}, \langref{FBDD} is the subset of \langref{uFBDD} with no $\vee$-nodes.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{FBDD}}
\target{\langref{OBDD}}
\status{\noquasi}
\begin{description}
The hidden weighted bit function $HWB_n$ has \langref{OBDD} size $2^{\Omega(n/4)}$ \citep{Bryant_1986} but \langref{FBDD} size $O(n^2)$ \citep{Gergov_1994}.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{FBDD}}
\target{\langref{dec-DNNF}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
Rewrite each decision node labeled $x$ as $(x \wedge D_0) \vee (\neg x \wedge D_1)$, where $D_0$ and $D_1$ are the rewritings of the 0- and 1-successors. The introduced $\vee$-gates are decision gates and the $\wedge$-gates are decomposable, so all $\vee$-gates arise from decision node rewrites, yielding a \langref{dec-DNNF} in linear time \citep{Amarilli_2018}.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{IP}}
\target{\langref{CNF}}
\status{\noquasi}
\begin{description}
Consider the \langref{DNF} formula $\Pi_n=\bigvee_{i=0}^{n-1}(x_{2i} \wedge x_{2i+1})$. This formula is in prime implicants form and each clause in $\Pi_n$ is an essential prime implicant of it. Hence its negation $\neg\Pi_n$ lies in \langref{IP}.

Since the early work of Quine, we know that the number of essential prime implicates of a formula is a lower bound on the number of clauses that can be found in any \langref{CNF} representation of it. However, since $\Pi_n$ has $2^n$ prime implicants, $\neg\Pi_n$ has $2^n$ prime implicates, so any \langref{CNF} representation of $\neg\Pi_n$ must have at least $2^n$ clauses. \citep{Darwiche_2002}
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{IP}}
\target{\langref{DNF}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
As defined by \citet{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{IP} is a subset of \langref{DNF}. Therefore, a polynomial compilation is trivial.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{IP}}
\target{\langref{FBDD}}
\status{\noquasi}
\begin{description}
The negation of the clique detection function $\neg\Sigma_{n,k}$ has $O(n^3)$ prime implicants (hence polynomial \langref{IP} size), but any \langref{FBDD} for $\neg\Sigma_{n,k}$ has exponential size for suitable $k$ \citep{Darwiche_2002,Wegener_1987}.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{IP}}
\target{\langref{MODS}}
\status{\noquasi}
\begin{description}
The $OR_n$ function $\bigvee_{i=1}^nx_i$ can be represented in $O(n)$ as a \langref{IP} formula but has $2^n-1$ models. \citep{Darwiche_2002}
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{MODS}}
\target{\langref{CNF}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
A Shannon tree for any \langref{MODS} formula can be generated in polynomial time. Paths from the root to 0-leaves yield a \langref{CNF} representation \citep{Darwiche_2002}.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{MODS}}
\target{\langref{d-DNNF}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
As defined by \citet{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{MODS} is a subset of \langref{d-DNNF}. Therefore, a polynomial compilation is trivial.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{MODS}}
\target{\langref{DNF}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
As defined by \citet{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{MODS} is a subset of \langref{DNF} (any \langref{MODS} formula can be written as a disjunction of the individual models). Therefore, a polynomial compilation is trivial.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{MODS}}
\target{\langref{IP}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
The Quine-McCluskey algorithm generates an \langref{IP} formula in polynomial time. \citep{Wegener_1987}
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{MODS}}
\target{\langfam{OBDD}{<}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
A Shannon tree for any \langref{MODS} formula following order $<$ can be generated in polynomial time. This tree can be reduced into a corresponding \langfam{OBDD}{<} in polynomial time \citep{Darwiche_2002}.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{MODS}}
\target{\langref{PI}}
\status{\nopolyunknownquasi}
\begin{description}
\citet{Kaleyski_2016} constructs a family of Boolean functions $\psi_i$ with \langref{MODS} size $\Theta(2^{2i})$ but \langref{PI} size $\Omega(2^{(i-1)^2/4})$, ruling out a polynomial compilation. Whether an exponential separation exists remains open.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{nFBDD}}
\target{\langref{d-DNNF}}
\status{\noquasi}
\begin{description}
The Sauerhoff function has an \langref{nFBDD} of size $O(n^2)$ over $n^2$ variables, but as a \langref{d-DNNF} has size $2^{\Omega(n)}$. \citep{Amarilli_2018}
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{nFBDD}}
\target{\langref{DNNF}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
Rewrite each decision node labeled $x$ as $(x \wedge D_0) \vee (\neg x \wedge D_1)$, where $D_0, D_1$ are the rewritings of the 0- and 1-successors. The $\wedge$-gates are decomposable, yielding a \langref{DNNF} in linear time \citep{Amarilli_2018}.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{nFBDD}}
\target{\langref{FBDD}}
\status{\noquasi}
\begin{description}
Consider the Boolean functions $\psi_n,\phi_n:\{0,1\}^{n^2}\to\{0,1\}$ that respectively test whether, in an $n \times n$ Boolean matrix, either the number of 1's is odd and there is a full row of 1's, or the number of 1's is even and there is a column full of 1's. An \langref{FBDD} of size $O(n^2)$ may simulate either $\psi_n$ or $\phi_n$, and thus the \langref{uFBDD} $\psi_n\vee\phi_n$ is of size $O(n^2)$; however, an \langref{FBDD} for $\psi_n\vee\phi_n$ is provably of size $2^{\Omega(n^{1/2})}$ \citep{Amarilli_2018}.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{nFBDD}}
\target{\langref{uFBDD}}
\status{\noquasi}
\begin{description}
The Sauerhoff function has an \langref{nFBDD} of size $O(n^2)$ over $n^2$ variables, but as a \langref{d-DNNF}, which generalize \langref{uFBDD}, has size $2^{\Omega(n)}$. \citep{Amarilli_2018}
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{nOBDD}}
\target{\langref{nFBDD}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
As defined by \citet{Amarilli_2018}, \langref{nOBDD} is the subset of \langref{nFBDD} satisfying an order of the variables, so compilation entails discarding the order.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{nOBDD}}
\target{\langref{SDNNF}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
Construct a right-linear v-tree from the variable order, then rewrite each decision node labeled $x$ as $(x \wedge D_0) \vee (\neg x \wedge D_1)$, where $D_0, D_1$ are the rewritings of the 0- and 1-successors. The v-tree alignment ensures every $\wedge$-gate respects the variable partition, yielding an \langref{SDNNF} in linear time \citep{Amarilli_2018}.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{OBDD}{<}}
\target{\langref{CNF}}
\status{\noquasi}
\begin{description}
The parity function $PARITY_n=\bigoplus_{i=1}^n x_i$ has linear size \langfam{OBDD}{<} representations but only exponential size \langref{CNF} representations. \citep{Darwiche_2002}
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{OBDD}{<}}
\target{\langref{DNF}}
\status{\noquasi}
\begin{description}
The parity function $PARITY_n=\bigoplus_{i=1}^n x_i$ has linear size \langfam{OBDD}{<} representations but only exponential size \langref{DNF} representations. \citep{Darwiche_2002}
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{OBDD}{<}}
\target{\langref{OBDD}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
As defined by \citet{Darwiche_2002}, \langfam{OBDD}{<} is a subset of \langref{OBDD}. Therefore, a polynomial compilation is trivial.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{OBDD}{<}}
\target{\langfam{TDD}{T}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
\citet[Theorem 17]{Capelli_2026} show that an \langfam{OBDD}{<} over order $\pi$ can be converted in linear time into an equivalent \langfam{TDD}{T} respecting the corresponding linear vtree $T_{\pi^{-1}}$, with size at most three times the input size.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{OBDD}{<}}
\target{\langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
Fix any variable order $<$ and let $T$ be the corresponding right-linear vtree. Rewriting each decision node labeled $x$ as $(x \wedge D_0) \vee (\neg x \wedge D_1)$ yields a structured output that respects $T$, and all introduced $\vee$-gates are decision gates, producing \langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T} in linear time. \citep{Amarilli_2018}
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{OBDD}{<}}
\target{\langfam{cSDD}{T}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
Fix any variable order $<$ and let $T$ be the corresponding right-linear vtree. Every \langfam{OBDD}{<} is a right-linear \langfam{SDD}{T}, and this representation is compressed (canonical), so compilation to \langfam{cSDD}{T} is polynomial-time. \citep{Darwiche_2011}
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{OBDD}{<}}
\target{\langfam{uOBDD}{<}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
For any fixed variable order $<$, every \langfam{OBDD}{<} is already a \langfam{uOBDD}{<} formula respecting the same order $<$. Thus, for each $<$, \langfam{OBDD}{<} $\subset$ \langfam{uOBDD}{<}, and the compilation is the identity. \citep{Amarilli_2018,Wegener_2000}
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{PI}}
\target{\langref{CNF}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
As defined by \citet{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{PI} is a subset of \langref{CNF}. Therefore, a polynomial compilation is trivial.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{PI}}
\target{\langref{DNF}}
\status{\noquasi}
\begin{description}
The Pairwise \langref{CNF} formula $\Sigma_n = \bigwedge_{i=0}^{n-1}(x_{2i} \vee x_{2i+1})$ has $2^n$ prime implicates; its negation $\neg\Sigma_n$ lies in \langref{PI} and therefore has $2^n$ essential prime implicants, forcing any \langref{DNF} representation to have at least $2^n$ terms \citep{Darwiche_2002}.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{PI}}
\target{\langref{DNNF}}
\status{\noquasi}
\begin{description}
\citet[Proposition 11]{Bova_2016} shows that the $JS_n$ has \langref{PI} size $O(n^2)$. \citet[Proposition 12]{Bova_2016} shows that $JS_n$ has \langref{DNNF} size $2^{\Omega(n^2)}$.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{PI}}
\target{\langref{FBDD}}
\status{\noquasi}
\begin{description}
The clique detection function $\Sigma_{n,k}$ has $O(n^3)$ prime implicates (hence polynomial \langref{PI} size), but any \langref{FBDD} for $\Sigma_{n,k}$ has exponential size for suitable $k$ \citep{Wegener_1987,Darwiche_2002}.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{PI}}
\target{\langref{MODS}}
\status{\noquasi}
\begin{description}
The $OR_n$ function $\bigvee_{i=1}^nx_i$ can be represented in $O(n)$ as a \langref{PI} formula but has $2^n-1$ models. \citep{Darwiche_2002}
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{SDD}}
\target{\langref{d-SDNNF}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
For each fixed vtree $T$, \langfam{SDD}{T} $\subset$ \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}, so compilation is trivial. \citep{VanDenBroeck_2015}
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{SDD}{T}}
\target{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
For any fixed vtree $T$, \langfam{SDD}{T} is a strict subset of \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}, so compilation is trivial. \citep{VanDenBroeck_2015}
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{SDD}{T}}
\target{\langref{FBDD}}
\status{\noquasi}
\begin{description}
By \citet[Corollary 3]{Bollig_2018}, the weighted sum function $WS_n$ can be represented by \langfam{SDD}{T}{s} of size $O(n^3)$ respecting the vtrees used in the construction, but has exponential \langref{FBDD} size. Hence \langfam{SDD}{T} does not compile to \langref{FBDD} in quasipolynomial size.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{SDD}{T}}
\target{\langref{SDD}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
\langref{SDD} is the union of the fixed-vtree languages \langfam{SDD}{T}. Hence any \langfam{SDD}{T} formula is already an \langref{SDD} formula, so the compilation is trivial and polynomial-time. \citep{Darwiche_2011}
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{SDD}{T}}
\target{\langfam{cSDD}{T}}
\status{\unknownboth}
\begin{description}
\citet[Theorem 1]{VanDenBroeck_2015} gives a class of Boolean functions $f_m$ and corresponding vtrees $T_m$ such that $f_m$ has an \langfam{SDD}{T_m} representation of size $O(m^2)$, yet the canonical \langfam{SDD}{T_m} representation of $f_m$ has size $\Omega(2^m)$. This separates uncompressed from compressed \langref{SDD}{s} when the vtree is held fixed. However, ruling out polynomial-size \langfam{cSDD}{T'} representations of these functions for every target vtree $T'$ remains open.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{SDNNF}}
\target{\langref{DNNF}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
As defined by \citet{Amarilli_2018}, a structured \langref{DNNF} is a triple of the corresponding \langref{DNNF}, the v-tree $T$, and a mapping $\rho$; as such, extracting a \langref{DNNF} from a \langref{SDNNF} is trivial.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{SDNNF}}
\target{\langref{nOBDD}}
\status{\unknownpolyquasi}
\begin{description}
\citet[Theorem 2]{Bollig_2018} shows that \langref{SDNNF} can be compiled into \langref{nOBDD} by adapting the compilation of \citet{Beame_2015} from \langref{DNNF} to \langref{nFBDD}.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{SDNNF}{T}}
\target{\langref{SDNNF}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
\langref{SDNNF} is the union of the fixed-vtree classes \langfam{SDNNF}{T}. Hence any \langfam{SDNNF}{T} formula is already an \langref{SDNNF} formula, so the compilation is trivial and polynomial-time. \citep{Pipatsrisawat_2008}
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{SDNNF}{T}}
\target{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}
\status{\unknownpolyquasi}
\begin{description}
The \langref{SDNNF}-to-\langref{nOBDD} simulation can be oriented as in \citet{TCZ_Forthcoming}: for every fixed source vtree $T$, it chooses one output order $<$ depending only on $T$ and gives a quasipolynomial-size \langfam{nOBDD}{<}.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{uFBDD}}
\target{\langref{d-DNNF}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
Rewrite each decision node labeled $x$ as $(x \wedge D_0) \vee (\neg x \wedge D_1)$, where $D_0, D_1$ are the rewritings of the 0- and 1-successors. The $\wedge$-gates are decomposable, and unambiguity of the \langref{uFBDD} ensures all introduced $\vee$-gates are deterministic, yielding a \langref{d-DNNF} in linear time \citep{Amarilli_2018}.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{uFBDD}}
\target{\langref{FBDD}}
\status{\noquasi}
\begin{description}
Consider the Boolean functions $\phi_n, \psi_n: \{0,1\}^{n^2}\to\{0,1\}$, which respectively test whether, in an $n\times n$ Boolean matrix, that the number of 1's is odd and there is a row full of 1's, and that the number of 1's is even and there is a column full of 1's. \citet{Bova_2016} and \citet{Wegener_2000} show that an \langref{FBDD} expression for $\phi_n \vee \psi_n$ has necessarily size $2^{\Omega(n^{1/2})}$; but since an \langref{FBDD} of size $O(n^2)$ may test $\phi_n$ or $\psi_n$ separately (shown by \citet[Proposition 3.1]{Amarilli_2018}), a \langref{uFBDD} for $\phi_n\vee \psi_n$ may be obtained by simply adding an $\vee$-gate joining these two \langref{FBDD} expressions, since $\phi_n \wedge \psi_n$ is unsatisfiable.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{uFBDD}}
\target{\langref{nFBDD}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
As defined by \citet{Amarilli_2018}, \langref{uFBDD} is the subset of \langref{nFBDD} satisfying unambiguity.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{uOBDD}}
\target{\langref{d-SDNNF}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
Construct a right-linear v-tree from the variable order, then rewrite each decision node labeled $x$ as $(x \wedge D_0) \vee (\neg x \wedge D_1)$, where $D_0, D_1$ are the rewritings of the 0- and 1-successors. The v-tree alignment ensures structuredness, and unambiguity of the \langref{uOBDD} makes all introduced $\vee$-gates deterministic, yielding a \langref{d-SDNNF} in linear time \citep{Amarilli_2018}.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{uOBDD}}
\target{\langref{FBDD}}
\status{\noquasi}
\begin{description}
The weighted sum function $WS_n$ has polynomial-size \langref{uOBDD} formulas: \citet{Wegener_2000} gives polynomial-size \langref{nOBDD}{<} formulas for $WS_n$ under every variable ordering $<$, and the pointer-guess construction has at most one accepting guess, so the formula is unambiguous. By \citet{Savicky_Zak_1996}, every \langref{FBDD} for $WS_n$ has size $2^{n-o(n)}$. Hence no quasipolynomial compilation from \langref{uOBDD} to \langref{FBDD} is possible.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{uOBDD}}
\target{\langref{nOBDD}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
As defined by \citet{Amarilli_2018}, \langref{uOBDD} is the subset of \langref{nOBDD} satisfying unambiguity.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{uOBDD}}
\target{\langref{uFBDD}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
As defined by \citet{Amarilli_2018}, \langref{uOBDD} is the subset of \langref{uFBDD} satisfying an order of the variables, so compilation entails discarding the order.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{uOBDD}}
\target{\langref{OBDD}}
\status{\noquasi}
\begin{description}
The hidden weighted bit function $HWB_n$ has polynomial-size \langref{uOBDD} representations under a fixed order $<$: $HWB_n = \bigvee_{k=1}^n(E_k^n(x) \wedge x_k)$, where each branch for $k$ tests the variables in the same order to verify the exact Hamming weight and $x_k=1$, and the branches are disjoint. Thus the representation is unambiguous and has polynomial size, as in the 2-\langref{OBDD}/\langref{FBDD} constructions discussed by \citet{Wegener_2000}. Bryant's hidden-weighted-bit lower bound says every \langref{OBDD} formula for $HWB_n$, regardless of variable order $<$, has exponential size \citep{Bryant_1986,Wegener_2000}. Hence no quasipolynomial compilation to \langref{OBDD} is possible.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{OBDD}}
\target{\langref{cSDD}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
As established by \citet{Darwiche_2011}, every \langref{OBDD} is a right-linear \langref{SDD}, which is already compressed (canonical). Thus, \langref{cSDD} strictly generalizes \langref{OBDD}.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{OBDD}}
\target{\langref{FBDD}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
As defined by \citet{Darwiche_2002}, \langref{OBDD} is a subset of \langref{FBDD}. Therefore a polynomial time compilation is trivial.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{OBDD}}
\target{\langfam{OBDD}{<}}
\status{\noquasi}
\begin{description}
Suppose an order $<$ existed so that \langref{OBDD} compiled to \langfam{OBDD}{<}. Take the first $2n$ variables in that order, call the first half $x_1,\ldots,x_n$ and the second half $y_1,\ldots,y_n$, and consider $EQ_n(x,y)$. This function has linear-size \langref{OBDD}{s} under the interleaved order $x_1,y_1,\ldots,x_n,y_n$, but exponential-size \langfam{OBDD}{<} representations \citep{Wegener_2000}. Thus no order $<$ can exist, and \langref{OBDD} is not quasipolynomial-time compilable to \langfam{OBDD}{<}.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{OBDD}}
\target{\langfam{SDNNF}{T}}
\status{\noquasi}
\begin{description}
For any fixed vtree $T$, a simple function can be chosen so that every \langfam{SDNNF}{T} representation is exponentially large, while some \langref{OBDD} represents it in linear size. Therefore \langref{OBDD} cannot compile to \langfam{SDNNF}{T} in quasipolynomial size \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{OBDD}}
\target{\langref{uOBDD}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
As defined by \citet{Amarilli_2018}, \langref{OBDD} is the subset of \langref{uOBDD} without any $\vee$-node.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{OBDD}}
\target{\langref{TDD}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
\citet[Theorem 17]{Capelli_2026} convert every fixed-order \langfam{OBDD}{<} into an equivalent \langfam{TDD}{T} of linear size for the corresponding linear vtree. Taking the union over orders gives a polynomial compilation from \langref{OBDD} to \langref{TDD}.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{OBDD}}
\target{\langref{dec-SDNNF}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
Construct a right-linear v-tree from the variable order, then rewrite each decision node labeled $x$ as $(x \wedge D_0) \vee (\neg x \wedge D_1)$, where $D_0, D_1$ are the rewritings of the 0- and 1-successors. The v-tree alignment ensures structuredness, and since all $\vee$-gates arise from decision node rewrites, every $\vee$-gate is a decision gate, yielding a \langref{dec-SDNNF} in linear time \citep{Amarilli_2018}.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{TDD}{T}}
\target{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
\citet[Theorem 3]{Capelli_2026} show that a \langfam{TDD}{T} can be converted in linear time into an equivalent \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} respecting the same vtree.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{TDD}{T}}
\target{\langfam{SDD}{T}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
\citet[Theorem 21]{Capelli_2026} show that, given a \langfam{TDD}{T} $C$ respecting a vtree $T$, one can construct a vtree $T'$ and an equivalent \langfam{SDD}{T'} $C'$ with $|C'| = O(|C|^2)$. This gives a polynomial compilation from \langfam{TDD}{T} to the corresponding fixed-vtree family \langfam{SDD}{T'}. Under our definition the output vtree $T'$ need not match the input vtree $T$.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{TDD}{T}}
\target{\langref{OBDD}}
\status{\nopolyunknownquasi}
\begin{description}
\citet[Theorem 14]{Capelli_2026} give polynomial-size \langfam{TDD}{T} representations for bounded-treewidth instances over the vtrees used in the construction. Combined with Razgon's \langref{OBDD} lower bound for bounded-treewidth CNFs, as observed by \citet{Capelli_2026}, this rules out polynomial compilation to \langref{OBDD}. The paper explicitly leaves quasipolynomial simulation by \langref{OBDD}{s} open.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{TDD}{T}}
\target{\langref{TDD}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
\langref{TDD} is the union of the fixed-vtree languages \langfam{TDD}{T}. Hence any \langfam{TDD}{T} representation is already a \langref{TDD} representation. \citep{Capelli_2026}
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{TDD}}
\target{\langref{d-SDNNF}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
\citet[Theorem 3]{Capelli_2026} convert every fixed-vtree TDD into an equivalent structured deterministic DNNF respecting the same vtree. Taking the union over vtrees gives a polynomial compilation from \langref{TDD} to \langref{d-SDNNF}.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{TDD}}
\target{\langref{SDD}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
\citet[Theorem 21]{Capelli_2026} show that, given a \langref{TDD} $C$ respecting a vtree $T$, one can construct a vtree $T'$ and an equivalent \langfam{SDD}{T'} $C'$ with $|C'| = O(|C|^2)$. Thus every \langref{TDD} can be converted into an equivalent polynomial-size \langref{SDD}.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{dec-DNNF}}
\target{\langref{d-DNNF}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
As defined by \citet{Amarilli_2018}, \langref{dec-DNNF} is the subset of \langref{d-DNNF} satisfying the decision property.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{dec-DNNF}}
\target{\langref{FBDD}}
\status{\unknownpolyquasi}
\begin{description}
\citet[Corollary 3.2]{Beame_2013} shows quasipolynomial compilation from \langref{dec-DNNF} to \langref{FBDD}.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{dec-SDNNF}}
\target{\langref{d-SDNNF}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
As defined by \citet{Amarilli_2018}, \langref{dec-SDNNF} is the subset of \langref{d-SDNNF} satisfying the decision property, so compilation is trivial.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{dec-SDNNF}}
\target{\langref{OBDD}}
\status{\unknownpolyquasi}
\begin{description}
\citet[Theorem 2]{Bollig_2018} shows that \langref{SDNNF} can be compiled into \langref{nOBDD} by adapting the compilation of \citet{Beame_2015} from \langref{DNNF} to \langref{nFBDD}; \citet[Proposition 2]{Bollig_2018} shows that this \langref{nOBDD} is unambiguous if the \langref{SDNNF} is deterministic. Additionally, the same compilation yields an \langref{OBDD} if the input is a \langref{dec-SDNNF} since there are no $\vee$-gates that are not decision gates in a \langref{dec-SDNNF}.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langref{dec-SDNNF}}
\target{\langref{dec-DNNF}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
As defined by \citet{Amarilli_2018}, a \langref{dec-SDNNF} is simply a structured \langref{dec-DNNF}, so discarding the v-tree completes the compilation.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T}}
\target{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
For any fixed vtree $T$, \langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T} is the subset of \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} satisfying the decision property, so compilation is trivial. \citep{Amarilli_2018}
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T}}
\target{\langref{nFBDD}}
\status{\nopolyunknownquasi}
\begin{description}
This is the fixed-vtree form of Proposition 3.10 of \citet{Amarilli_2018}.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T}}
\target{\langfam{OBDD}{<}}
\status{\unknownpolyquasi}
\begin{description}
The common-order quasipolynomial simulation of \citet{TCZ_Forthcoming} specializes to \langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T}: for every fixed source vtree $T$, it chooses one output order $<$ depending only on $T$ and produces a quasipolynomial-size \langfam{OBDD}{<}.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T}}
\target{\langref{dec-SDNNF}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
\langref{dec-SDNNF} is the union of fixed-vtree \langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T} languages. Hence any fixed-class formula is already a \langref{dec-SDNNF} formula, so the compilation is trivial and polynomial-time. \citep{Amarilli_2018}
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{cSDD}{T}}
\target{\langref{cSDD}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
\langref{cSDD} is the union of the fixed-vtree languages \langfam{cSDD}{T}. Hence any \langfam{cSDD}{T} formula is already a \langref{cSDD} formula, so the compilation is trivial and polynomial-time. \citep{Darwiche_2011}
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{cSDD}{T}}
\target{\langfam{OBDD}{<}}
\status{\noquasi}
\begin{description}
\citet{Bova_2016_a} gives functions $F_n$ with polynomial-size \langfam{cSDD}{T} representations over explicit vtrees. Conditioning the auxiliary variables of $F_n$ recovers $HWB_n$, and conditioning a \langfam{OBDD}{<} preserves the order and cannot increase size. Since \citet{Bryant_1986,Wegener_2000} give exponential \langfam{OBDD}{<} size for $HWB_n$ for every order, every \langfam{OBDD}{<} for $F_n$ is exponential. Hence \langfam{cSDD}{T} is not quasipolynomial-time compilable to \langfam{OBDD}{<}.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{cSDD}{T}}
\target{\langfam{SDD}{T}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
For any fixed vtree $T$, \langfam{cSDD}{T} is the compressed subset of \langfam{SDD}{T}, so compilation is trivial. \citep{VanDenBroeck_2015}
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{cSDD}{T}}
\target{\langref{OBDD}}
\status{\noquasi}
\begin{description}
\citet[Theorem 4]{Bova_2016_a} constructs a generalized hidden weighted bit function $F_n$ with polynomial-size \langfam{cSDD}{T} representations respecting explicit vtrees, but every \langref{OBDD} for $F_n$ has size $2^{\Omega(n)}$. Hence \langfam{cSDD}{T} is not quasipolynomial-time compilable to \langref{OBDD}.
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}
\target{\langref{nOBDD}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
\langref{nOBDD} is the union of fixed-order languages \langfam{nOBDD}{<}. Hence any \langfam{nOBDD}{<} formula is already an \langref{nOBDD} formula, so the compilation is trivial and polynomial-time. \citep{Wegener_2000}
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}
\target{\langfam{SDNNF}{T}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
Fix any variable order $<$ and let $T$ be the corresponding right-linear vtree. Rewriting each decision node labeled $x$ as $(x \wedge D_0) \vee (\neg x \wedge D_1)$ yields an output respecting $T$, so \langfam{nOBDD}{<} compiles to \langfam{SDNNF}{T} in linear time. \citep{Amarilli_2018}
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{uOBDD}{<}}
\target{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
Fix any variable order $<$ and let $T$ be the corresponding right-linear vtree. Rewriting each decision node labeled $x$ as $(x \wedge D_0) \vee (\neg x \wedge D_1)$ yields a structured output respecting $T$, and unambiguity ensures determinism of introduced $\vee$-gates, giving \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} in linear time. \citep{Amarilli_2018}
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{uOBDD}{<}}
\target{\langref{uOBDD}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
\langref{uOBDD} is the union of fixed-order languages \langfam{uOBDD}{<}. Hence any \langfam{uOBDD}{<} formula is already a \langref{uOBDD} formula, so the compilation is trivial and polynomial-time. \citep{Wegener_2000}
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}

\begin{succinctnessclaim}
\source{\langfam{uOBDD}{<}}
\target{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}
\status{\poly}
\begin{description}
For any fixed variable order $<$, every \langfam{uOBDD}{<} formula is a \langfam{nOBDD}{<} formula respecting the same order $<$. Thus, for each $<$, \langfam{uOBDD}{<} $\subset$ \langfam{nOBDD}{<}, and the compilation is the identity. \citep{Amarilli_2018,Wegener_2000}
\end{description}
\end{succinctnessclaim}
\bibliographystyle{plainnat}
\bibliography{refs}
\end{document}
